text: If all mammals are warm-blooded, and a whale is a mammal, then a whale is warm-blooded. Modus ponens applied.
Premise one: All birds have feathers. Premise two: A penguin is a bird. Conclusion: A penguin has feathers.
If P implies Q and Q implies R, then P implies R. This is the law of hypothetical syllogism.
Modus tollens: If it rains then the ground is wet. The ground is not wet. Therefore it is not raining.
Disjunctive syllogism: Either A or B is true. A is false. Therefore B is true.
If all cats are animals and no animals are plants, then no cats are plants. Universal syllogism applied.
Contrapositive: If P then Q is equivalent to if not Q then not P. Both carry the same truth value.
De Morgan's first law: Not A and not B is equivalent to not the proposition A or B.
De Morgan's second law: Not A or not B is equivalent to not the proposition A and B.
A biconditional P if and only if Q means P implies Q and Q implies P both hold simultaneously.
If exactly one of A or B is true (XOR), then A and B have different truth values.
Puzzle: Alice always tells the truth. Bob always lies. Alice says Bob is a liar. Since Alice tells truth, Bob is indeed a liar. Consistent.
Puzzle: Three boxes contain apples, oranges, or both. All labels are wrong. The label 'apples and oranges' must contain only one type. Opening it determines all three.
If no X is Y and some Z are X, then some Z are not Y. Valid syllogism by contradiction.
A tautology is true in all possible truth-value assignments. P or not P is always true regardless of P.
A contradiction is false in all possible assignments. P and not P is always false regardless of P.
Puzzle: Five people sit in a row. Alice is not next to Bob. Carol sits at one end. Dave is between Eve and Carol. Determine positions from constraints.
Proof by contradiction: Assume the conclusion is false. Derive a logical impossibility. Therefore the conclusion must be true.
Universal instantiation: If all x satisfy predicate P, then any specific element a also satisfies P.
Existential generalisation: If a specific element a satisfies predicate P, then there exists some x satisfying P.
Puzzle: A farmer has chickens and rabbits. There are 20 heads and 56 legs. Eight rabbits and twelve chickens satisfy both constraints.
If premises lead to a contradiction, at least one premise is false. This is the basis of reductio ad absurdum.
Conjunction elimination: If A and B is true, then A is true and B is true separately.
Conjunction introduction: If A is true and B is true independently, then A and B is true.
Disjunction introduction: If A is true, then A or B is true for any B, since at least one disjunct is satisfied.
Puzzle: In a room of 23 people, the probability that two share a birthday exceeds 50 percent. This is the birthday paradox.
Implication is false only when the antecedent is true and the consequent is false.
Puzzle: Knights always tell truth, knaves always lie. A says we are both knaves. A must be a knave, B must be a knight.
Resolution rule: From A or B and not A or C, conclude B or C. Fundamental in automated theorem proving.
Puzzle: Alice, Bob, Carol are doctors, lawyers, engineers. Alice is not a doctor. Bob is not a lawyer. Carol is not an engineer. Use process of elimination.
If A implies B and A implies not B, then A must be false by explosion combined with consistency.
Abductive reasoning: The ground is wet. Rain is the simplest explanation. Therefore it probably rained. This is inference to the best explanation.
Puzzle: A man has two coins totalling 30 cents. One is not a nickel. The other coin is a nickel. The first coin is a quarter.
Inductive reasoning: All observed ravens are black. This is evidence but not proof that all ravens are black.
Puzzle: A clock shows 3:15. The angle between the hour and minute hands is 7.5 degrees, not zero.
If P is necessary for Q, then Q cannot be true without P. Necessity constrains possibility.
If P is sufficient for Q, then P alone guarantees Q regardless of anything else.
Puzzle: Find two natural numbers whose sum is 11 and product is 24. The answer is 3 and 8.
A valid argument preserves truth from premises to conclusion. A sound argument is valid and has true premises.
Puzzle: A boat has a ladder with rungs 30 cm apart. The bottom rung is at water level. As the tide rises 90 cm, two rungs are submerged because the boat rises with the tide.
Propositional calculus treats sentences as atomic units. Predicate calculus decomposes sentences into subjects and predicates.
If A is both necessary and sufficient for B then A if and only if B. The conditions are interchangeable.

Puzzle: You have a 3-litre and 5-litre jug. Fill the 5-litre, pour into 3-litre, leaving 2 litres. Empty the 3-litre. Pour 2 litres in. Fill 5-litre again. Pour 1 litre into 3-litre. Result: 4 litres in 5-litre jug.
Zeno's paradox: Achilles gives a tortoise a head start. Each time Achilles reaches where the tortoise was, it has moved ahead. Yet convergent series shows Achilles overtakes.
Logical equivalence: Two statements are equivalent when they have the same truth table.
Puzzle: Three missionaries and three cannibals cross a river in a boat holding two. Never let cannibals outnumber missionaries on either bank. Solve with six steps.
Existential instantiation: From there exists x satisfying P, we may name that x and work with it, but cannot overgeneralise.
Puzzle: A store sells red and blue balls. If you pick three at random you always get at least one red and one blue. The drawer has exactly two of each colour.
Double negation: Not not P is equivalent to P in classical logic but not in intuitionistic logic.
Puzzle: Four people must cross a bridge at night with one torch. They cross in pairs at the speed of the slower person. Crossing times 1, 2, 5, 10. Minimum total time is 17 minutes.
